text:
{
"text": "Apart from light microscopes, scientists also use powerful electron microscopes that reveal the fine details of a cell structure. These instruments use a beam of electrons instead of light to produce highly magnified images, allowing us to see cell structure at the nanometre scale with remarkable clarity (a nanometre is one-billionth of a metre).\n\nActivity 2.1: Let us estimate the size of a cell\n1. Take a transparent ruler with millimetre (mm) markings.\n2. Place the ruler on the stage of microscope, focus on it using the adjustment knob and observe the diameter of the circular field of view through the eyepiece and measure it in mm.\n3. Convert the diameter from mm to micrometre ($\mu$m). Suppose the diameter of the visible field is 5 mm, meaning 5 $\times$ 1000 = 5000 $\mu$m.\n4. Remove the ruler and place an onion peel slide on the stage of the microscope.\n5. Focus on the slide and count the number of cells present along the diameter of the field of view in one straight line.\n6. Estimate the real size of the cell using the formula:\n\nSuppose, 25 cells are seen along the diameter. In that case, the size of one onion cell would be 500 $\mu$m / 25 = 20 $\mu$m.\n\nIf the estimated size of an onion peel cell is 200 $\mu$m, how much does a light microscope magnify this cell? The total magnification of a microscope depends on its magnifying power of the eyepiece and the objective lens. If both the eyepiece and the objective lens have the magnifying power of 10X, then the total magnification will be 100X. This means that a cell with an estimated size of 200 $\mu$m will appear 100 times larger.\n\nThus, a microscope allows us to see very small structures clearly and is an essential tool for studying the cell structure. Over the years, scientists have improved the microscope by improving its three main features — resolution (measure of clarity), contrast (the difference in brightness between various",
